topic: Two siblings inherit their grandmother's house
raw_output:
[story 1]

The sheer scale of Grandmother Elara’s estate hit Liam like a physical blow. Dust motes danced in the shafts of sunlight slicing through the tall, arched windows of the foyer, illuminating the absurdly ornate, yet undeniably dusty, furnishings. He clutched the brittle deed in his hand, his knuckles white. "Well," he managed, his voice sounding unnaturally high in the cavernous space, "this is… a lot." He shifted his weight from one foot to the other, acutely aware of the way his borrowed, slightly too-tight blazer felt against his skin. He desperately wanted to find a corner, a quiet place to disappear, but every room seemed equally imposing, demanding attention. He cleared his throat, attempting to summon a tone of casual proprietorship, but it came out thin and reedy.

[story 2]

Maya surveyed the drawing-room, her gaze lingering on the faded velvet drapes and the portrait of a stern-looking ancestor. A flush crept up her neck, warming her cheeks instantly. She felt every eye in the house—even the silent, dusty objects—focused on her. "I suppose," she murmured, her tone deliberately flat, trying to sound as if she were entirely unperturbed by the monumental task ahead, "we'll have to sort through this." She immediately began fiddling with the hem of her dress, pulling the fabric down over her knees with unnecessary vigor. Every movement felt clumsy, a performance she hadn't rehearsed. She longed for the simple anonymity of the city, anywhere but this echoing mausoleum of forgotten grandeur.

[story 3]

When Ben first stepped into the kitchen, the smell of aged wood and lavender overwhelmed him. He stopped dead in the doorway, his jaw tightening almost imperceptibly. He was acutely aware of his slightly rumpled shirt and the way his hair seemed to stick to his forehead under the oppressive silence. "Right," he announced, the word sounding brittle. He took a deep, shaky breath, trying to project an air of competence, but his hands began to tremble slightly as he gestured vaguely toward a towering stack of antique china. He wanted nothing more than to vanish behind the pantry door, to become invisible amidst the overwhelming evidence of their inheritance.

When Ben first stepped into the kitchen, the smell of aged wood and lavender overwhelmed him. He stopped dead in the doorway, his jaw tightening almost imperceptibly.
